1.1: There was a man in the land of Uz whose name was Job, and that man was blameless and upright, and one who feared God and turned away from evil.
1.2: And there were born to him seven sons and three daughters.
1.3: His possessions were seven thousand sheep and three thousand camels and five hundred yoke of oxen and five hundred she-donkeys, and a very great household, and that man was the greatest of all the people of the east.
1.4: And his sons went and made a feast in the house of each one on his day, and they sent and called their three sisters to eat and to drink with them.
1.5: And when the days of feasting had run their course, Job would send and consecrate them, and he would rise early in the morning and offer burnt offerings according to the number of them all. For Job said, 'Perhaps my sons have sinned and cursed God in their hearts.' Thus Job did continually.
1.6: Now there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and the Adversary also came among them.
1.7: And the LORD said to the Adversary, 'From where have you come?' The Adversary answered the LORD and said, 'From going to and fro in the earth and from walking up and down in it.'
1.8: And the LORD said to the Adversary, 'Have you considered my servant Job? There is no one like him on earth — a blameless and upright man who fears God and turns away from evil.'
1.9: Then the Adversary answered the LORD and said, 'Does Job fear God for nothing?'
1.10: Have you not made a hedge around him and around his house and around all that he has on every side? You have blessed the work of his hands, and his possessions have increased in the land.
1.11: But put forth your hand now and touch all that he has, and he will curse you to your face.
1.12: And the LORD said to the Adversary, 'Behold, all that he has is in your hand; only do not stretch out your hand against him.' So the Adversary went out from the presence of the LORD.
1.13: Now there was a day when his sons and his daughters were eating and drinking wine in the house of their eldest brother.
1.14: And a messenger came to Job and said, 'The oxen were plowing and the donkeys were feeding beside them,'
1.15: '— and the Sabeans fell upon them and seized them; they have struck down the servants with the edge of the sword, and I alone have escaped to tell you.'
1.16: While he was yet speaking, there came also another and said, 'The fire of God fell from heaven and burned up the sheep and the servants and consumed them, and I alone have escaped to tell you.'
1.17: While he was yet speaking, there came also another and said, 'The Chaldeans made out three bands and fell upon the camels and carried them away, and also struck down the servants with the edge of the sword; and I alone have escaped to tell you.'
1.18: While he was yet speaking, there came also another and said, 'Your sons and your daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother's house,'
1.19: '— and behold, a great wind came from across the wilderness and struck the four corners of the house, and it fell upon the young men, and they are dead; and I alone have escaped to tell you.'
1.20: Then Job arose and rent his mantle and shaved his head and fell upon the ground and worshipped.
1.21: And he said, 'Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked shall I return there. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD.'
1.22: In all this Job sinned not, nor charged God with foolishness.

2.1: Again there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and the Adversary also came among them to present himself before the LORD.
2.2: And the LORD said to the Adversary, 'From where have you come?' The Adversary answered the LORD and said, 'From going to and fro in the earth and from walking up and down in it.'
2.3: And the LORD said to the Adversary, 'Have you considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth — a blameless and upright man who fears God and turns away from evil? And he still holds fast to his integrity, although you incited me against him to destroy him without cause.'
2.4: And the Adversary answered the LORD and said, 'Skin for skin! All that a man has he will give for his life.'
2.5: But put forth your hand now and touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse you to your face.
2.6: And the LORD said to the Adversary, 'Behold, he is in your hand; only preserve his life.'
2.7: So the Adversary went out from the presence of the LORD and struck Job with loathsome boils from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head.
2.8: And he took himself a potsherd to scrape himself withal, and he sat down among the ashes.
2.9: Then his wife said to him, 'Do you still hold fast your integrity? Curse God and die.'
2.10: But he said to her, 'You speak as one of the foolish women speaks. Shall we receive good from God and shall we not receive evil?' In all this Job sinned not with his lips.
2.11: Now when Job's three friends heard of all this evil that had come upon him — Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite — they came every one from his own place and met together to mourn with him and to comfort him.
2.12: And when they lifted up their eyes from afar and did not recognize him, they lifted up their voice and wept, and they rent every man his mantle and sprinkled dust upon their heads toward heaven.
2.13: And they sat down with him upon the ground seven days and seven nights, and none spoke a word to him, for they saw that his grief was very great.

3.1: After this Job opened his mouth and cursed his day.
3.3: Let the day perish on which I was born, and the night that said, 'A man is conceived.'
3.4: Let that day be darkness! Let not God above inquire after it, and let no light shine upon it.
3.5: Let darkness and the shadow of death claim it; let a cloud dwell upon it; let the blackness of the day terrify it.
3.6: As for that night — let thick darkness seize it; let it not rejoice among the days of the year; let it not come into the number of the months.
3.7: Lo, let that night be desolate; let no joyful voice come into it.
3.8: Let them curse it who curse the day — those who are ready to rouse up Leviathan.
3.9: Let the stars of its dawn be dark; let it look for light but have none, and let it not see the dawning of the day.
3.10: Because it did not shut the doors of my mother's womb, nor hide sorrow from my eyes.
3.11: Why did I not die from the womb? Why did I not give up the ghost when I came out of the belly?
3.12: Why did the knees receive me? Or why the breasts, that I should suck?
3.13: For now should I have lain still and been quiet; I should have slept; then had I been at rest,
3.14: with kings and counsellors of the earth who built desolate places for themselves,
3.15: or with princes who had gold, who filled their houses with silver.
3.16: Or as a hidden untimely birth I had not been, as infants that never saw light.
3.17: There the wicked cease from troubling, and there the weary are at rest.
3.18: There the prisoners rest together; they hear not the voice of the oppressor.
3.19: The small and the great are there, and the servant is free from his master.
3.20: Why is light given to him that is in misery, and life to the bitter in soul?
3.21: who long for death but it comes not, and who search for it more than for hidden treasures,
3.22: who rejoice exceedingly and are glad when they can find the grave?
3.23: Why is light given to a man whose way is hidden, and whom God has hedged in?
3.24: For my sighing comes before my food, and my roarings are poured out like the waters.
3.25: For the thing which I greatly feared has come upon me, and that which I was afraid of has come unto me.
3.26: I was not at ease, neither had I rest, neither was I quiet, yet trouble came.

4.1: Then answered Eliphaz the Temanite, and said,
4.2: If one ventures a word with you, will you be impatient? Yet who can withhold himself from speaking?
4.3: Behold, you have instructed many, and the weak hands you have strengthened.
4.4: The stumbling one your words have upheld, and the faltering knees you have made firm.
4.5: But now it comes upon you, and you are impatient; it touches you, and you are dismayed.
4.6: Is not your fear your confidence, and the integrity of your ways your hope?
4.7: Remember, I pray: who that was innocent has perished? Or where have the upright been cut off?
4.8: As I have seen, those who plow iniquity and those who sow wickedness reap it.
4.9: By the breath of God they perish, and by the blast of his nostrils they are consumed.
4.10: The roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion — the teeth of the young lions are broken.
4.11: The strong lion perishes for lack of prey, and the whelps of the lioness are scattered abroad.
4.12: Now a word was secretly brought to me, and my ear received a whisper of it.
4.13: In thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep falls on men,
4.14: fear came upon me and trembling, which made all my bones shake.
4.15: A spirit passed before my face; the hair of my flesh stood on end.
4.16: It stood still, but I could not discern its form. A shape was before my eyes; silence, then I heard a voice:
4.17: 'Can a mortal man be more righteous than God? Can a man be purer than his Maker?'
4.18: 'Behold, in his servants he places no trust, and his angels he charges with folly.'
4.19: 'How much more those who dwell in clay houses, whose foundation is in the dust, who are crushed before the moth!'
4.20: 'From morning to evening they are shattered; without regard, they perish forever.'
4.21: 'Is not their tent cord plucked from within them? They die — and without wisdom.'

5.1: Call now; is there one who will answer you? And to which of the holy ones will you turn?
5.2: For vexation kills the foolish man, and envy slays the simple.
5.3: I have seen the foolish taking root, and suddenly I cursed his dwelling.
5.4: His children are far from safety; they are crushed in the gate, and there is none to deliver.
5.5: The hungry devour his harvest, taking it from among the thorns, and the snare swallows his wealth.
5.6: For affliction does not come forth from the dust, nor does trouble spring from the ground,
5.7: but man is born to trouble as the sparks fly upward.
5.8: But I would seek God, and to God would I commit my cause,
5.9: who does great things and unsearchable, marvellous things without number:
5.10: who gives rain upon the earth and sends waters upon the fields;
5.11: who sets on high those that be low, that those who mourn may be exalted to safety;
5.12: who frustrates the devices of the crafty, so that their hands cannot succeed;
5.13: he catches the wise in their own craftiness, and the counsel of the wily is carried headlong.
5.14: They meet with darkness in the daytime and grope at noon as in the night.
5.15: But he saves the needy from the sword of their mouth and from the hand of the mighty.
5.16: So the poor have hope, and injustice shuts her mouth.
5.17: Behold, happy is the man whom God corrects; therefore do not despise the discipline of the Almighty.
5.18: For he wounds, but he also binds up; he strikes, but his hands heal.
5.19: In six troubles he will deliver you, and in seven no evil shall touch you.
5.20: In famine he will redeem you from death, and in war from the power of the sword.
5.21: You will be hidden from the scourge of the tongue, and you will not fear when destruction comes.
5.22: At destruction and famine you shall laugh, and you shall not fear the wild beasts of the earth.
5.23: For you shall be in covenant with the stones of the field, and the beasts of the field shall be at peace with you.
5.24: You shall know that your tent is at peace, and you shall visit your fold and lack nothing.
5.25: You shall know also that your offspring shall be many, and your descendants as the grass of the earth.
5.26: You shall come to your grave in full vigor, as a shock of grain comes up in its season.
5.27: Behold, this we have searched out; it is true. Hear it and know it for yourself.

6.2: Oh that my vexation were weighed, and all my calamity laid in the balances!
6.3: For then it would be heavier than the sand of the seas; therefore my words have been reckless.
6.4: For the arrows of the Almighty are in me; my spirit drinks their poison; the terrors of God are arrayed against me.
6.5: Does the wild donkey bray when it has grass, or does the ox low when it has its fodder?
6.6: Can tasteless food be eaten without salt, or is there any flavor in the white of an egg?
6.7: My appetite refuses to touch them; they are as sickness to me.
6.8: Oh that I might have my request, and that God would grant my desire:
6.9: that it would please God to crush me, that he would let loose his hand and cut me off!
6.10: This would be my comfort; I would even exult in unsparing pain, for I have not denied the words of the Holy One.
6.11: What is my strength, that I should wait? And what is my end, that I should prolong my life?
6.12: Is my strength the strength of stones? Is my flesh of bronze?
6.13: Is it not that there is no help in me, and that sound wisdom is driven from me?
6.14: To the despairing, steadfast love from his friend — else he forsakes the fear of the Almighty.
6.15: My brothers have dealt treacherously like a desert stream, like channels of streams that pass away,
6.16: which are black from the ice, in which the snow is hidden;
6.17: when the heat comes, they are gone; when it is hot, they dry up from their place.
6.18: The paths of their course wind away; they go up into emptiness and are lost.
6.19: The caravans of Tema looked; the companies of Sheba waited and hoped.
6.20: They are confounded because they had hoped; they come there and are ashamed.
6.21: For now you have become nothing; you see my terror and are afraid.
6.22: Have I said, 'Give to me,' or, 'Offer a bribe for me from your wealth'?
6.23: Or, 'Deliver me from the hand of the enemy'? Or, 'Redeem me from the hand of the ruthless'?
6.24: Teach me, and I will be silent; and make me to understand wherein I have erred.
6.25: How forceful are upright words! But what does your reproving prove?
6.26: Do you think to reprove words, when the speech of a desperate man is but wind?
6.27: You would even cast lots over the fatherless and barter over your friend.
6.28: But now, be pleased to look at me, for I will not lie to your face.
6.29: Return, I pray; let no injustice be done; yea, return again — my righteousness is still intact.
6.30: Is there injustice on my tongue? Cannot my palate discern what is wrong?

7.1: Is there not appointed service for a mortal on earth? Are not his days like the days of a hired laborer?
7.2: Like a servant who pants for the shadow, and like a hired laborer who looks for his wages —
7.3: So I am made to inherit months of emptiness, and nights of trouble are appointed to me.
7.4: When I lie down I say, 'When shall I arise?' The night drags on, and I am filled with tossings until the dawn.
7.5: My flesh is clothed with worms and clods of dust; my skin breaks open and runs.
7.6: My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle and come to an end without hope.
7.7: Remember that my life is a breath; my eye will see good no more.
7.8: The eye of him who sees me will see me no more; your eyes will look upon me, but I will not be.
7.9: As a cloud fades away and vanishes, so one who goes down to Sheol does not come up.
7.10: He does not return to his house, nor does his place know him anymore.
7.11: Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.
7.12: Am I the sea, or a sea monster, that you have set a watch over me?
7.13: When I say, 'My couch will comfort me, my bed will ease my complaint' —
7.14: — then you frighten me with dreams and terrify me with visions,
7.15: so that my soul would choose strangling, and death rather than my bones.
7.16: I loathe it; I would not live forever. Let me alone, for my days are a breath.
7.17: What is man, that you make so much of him, and that you set your heart upon him —
7.18: — visiting him every morning and testing him every moment?
7.19: How long will you not look away from me and not let me alone until I swallow my saliva?
7.20: If I have sinned, what have I done to you, O watcher of men? Why have you made me your target, so that I am a burden to myself?
7.21: Why do you not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For now I shall lie down in the dust, and you will seek me earnestly, but I shall not be.

8.1: Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
8.2: How long will you speak these things? And how long shall the words of your mouth be a mighty wind?
8.3: Does God pervert justice? Does the Almighty pervert righteousness?
8.4: If your children sinned against him, he delivered them into the power of their transgression.
8.5: If you will seek God earnestly and make supplication to the Almighty —
8.6: if you are pure and upright, surely then he will rouse himself for you and restore your righteous dwelling —
8.7: though your beginning was small, your latter end shall greatly increase.
8.8: For please inquire of the former age and attend to the things their fathers searched out —
8.9: for we are but of yesterday and know nothing, for our days on earth are a shadow.
8.10: Will they not teach you and tell you, and utter words from their understanding?
8.11: Can papyrus grow tall where there is no marsh? Can reeds flourish without water?
8.12: While still in its greenness, not yet cut down, it withers before any other plant.
8.13: Such are the paths of all who forget God; the hope of the godless will perish.
8.14: His confidence is cut off, and his trust is a spider's house.
8.15: He leans on his house, but it does not stand; he holds fast to it, but it does not endure.
8.16: He is well watered before the sun, and his shoots spread over his garden.
8.17: His roots are entwined around the stone heap; he gazes upon a house of stones.
8.18: If he is swept from his place, then it denies him: 'I have not seen you.'
8.19: Behold, this is the joy of his way, and from the earth others will spring up.
8.20: Behold, God will not reject a blameless person, nor will he take the hand of evildoers.
8.21: He will yet fill your mouth with laughter and your lips with shouts of joy.
8.22: Those who hate you will be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked will be no more.

9.2: Truly I know it is so. But how can a mortal be in the right before God?
9.3: If one wished to contend with him, one could not answer him one out of a thousand times.
9.4: He is wise in heart and mighty in strength — who has hardened himself against him and come out unscathed?
9.5: He who removes mountains, and they know it not, when he overturns them in his anger —
9.6: who shakes the earth out of its place, so that its pillars tremble —
9.7: who commands the sun and it does not rise, and seals up the stars —
9.8: who alone stretched out the heavens and treads on the heights of the sea —
9.9: who made the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades and the chambers of the south —
9.10: who does great things past finding out, and marvelous things without number.
9.11: Lo, he passes by me, and I do not see him; he moves on, and I do not perceive him.
9.12: Behold, he seizes — who can turn him back? Who will say to him, 'What are you doing?'
9.13: God will not turn back his anger; beneath him the helpers of Rahab bowed down.
9.14: How then can I answer him, or choose out my words with him?
9.15: Though I were righteous, I could not answer him; I could only implore grace from my judge.
9.16: If I called and he answered me, I would not believe he was truly listening to my voice.
9.17: For he crushes me with a tempest and multiplies my wounds without cause.
9.18: He will not let me recover my breath, but fills me with bitterness.
9.19: If it is strength — behold, he is mighty! If it is justice — who will summon him?
9.20: Though I were righteous, my own mouth would condemn me; though I were blameless, he would prove me perverse.
9.21: I am blameless; I do not know my own self; I despise my life.
9.22: It is all one; therefore I say: he destroys both the blameless and the wicked.
9.23: When disaster suddenly kills, he mocks at the despair of the innocent.
9.24: The earth is given into the hand of the wicked; he covers the faces of its judges. If not he, then who?
9.25: Now my days are swifter than a runner; they flee away and see no good.
9.26: They pass by like boats of reed, like an eagle swooping on its prey.
9.27: If I say, 'I will forget my complaint, I will set aside my sad face, and be cheerful' —
9.28: I am afraid of all my suffering, for I know you will not hold me innocent.
9.29: I shall be found wicked; why then do I labor in vain?
9.30: If I wash myself with snow water and cleanse my hands with lye —
9.31: — then you would plunge me into a pit, so that my own garments would abhor me.
9.32: For he is not a man as I am, that I could answer him, that we might come to court together.
9.33: There is no arbiter between us who might lay his hand upon us both.
9.34: Let him take his rod away from me, and let not the terror of him terrify me.
9.35: Then I would speak, and I would not fear him, for I am not so within myself.

10.1: My soul loathes my life; I will give loose rein to my complaint; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
10.2: I will say to God: Do not condemn me; show me why you contend with me.
10.3: Is it good to you to oppress, to despise the work of your hands, and to shine upon the counsel of the wicked?
10.4: Have you eyes of flesh? Or do you see as a mortal sees?
10.5: Are your days like the days of a mortal, or your years like the years of a man?
10.6: that you search out my iniquity and inquire after my sin —
10.7: though you know I am not wicked, and there is none who can deliver from your hand?
10.8: Your hands fashioned and made me altogether, and yet you are destroying me.
10.9: Remember that you made me like clay; will you return me again to dust?
10.10: Did you not pour me out like milk and curdle me like cheese?
10.11: You clothed me with skin and flesh, and wove me together with bones and sinews.
10.12: You granted me life and covenant love, and your visitation has preserved my spirit.
10.13: Yet these things you hid in your heart; I know that this was your intent.
10.14: If I sin, you mark it, and you will not acquit me of my iniquity.
10.15: If I am wicked, woe to me! If I am righteous, I dare not lift up my head, for I am filled with shame and sated with my affliction.
10.16: And if I lift myself up, you hunt me like a lion and again display your marvels against me.
10.17: You renew your witnesses against me and increase your vexation toward me; wave upon wave of trouble and a host of hardships assail me.
10.18: Why then did you bring me out from the womb? Would that I had died and no eye had seen me!
10.19: I should have been as though I had never been; I should have been carried from the womb to the grave.
10.20: Are not my days few? Let him cease, so I may take a little comfort before I go —
10.21: — before I go to the land of no return, the land of darkness and the shadow of death —
10.22: — a land of thick gloom as darkness itself, of the shadow of death and disorder, where the light is like darkness.

11.1: Then Zophar the Naamathite answered and said:
11.2: Shall a multitude of words go unanswered, and a man of many lips be justified?
11.3: Shall your empty talk silence men? When you mock, shall no man put you to shame?
11.4: For you have said, 'My teaching is pure, and I am clean in your eyes.'
11.5: But oh, that God would speak and open his lips against you —
11.6: and tell you the secrets of wisdom, for it has many sides. Know that God has exacted of you less than your iniquity deserves.
11.7: Can you find out the deep things of God? Can you reach the limit of Shaddai?
11.8: Higher than the heavens — what can you do? Deeper than Sheol — what can you know?
11.9: Its measure is longer than the earth and broader than the sea.
11.10: If he passes through and imprisons and calls an assembly, who can hinder him?
11.11: For he knows hollow men; he sees wickedness — does he not then observe it?
11.12: But a hollow man will get understanding when a wild donkey's foal is born a man.
11.13: If you set your heart straight and stretch out your hands to him —
11.14: if wickedness is in your hand, put it far away, and let not injustice dwell in your tents —
11.15: then truly you will lift up your face without blemish, and you will be firm and unafraid.
11.16: For you will forget your misery; you will remember it as waters that have passed away.
11.17: And your life will be brighter than the noonday; your darkness will be like the morning.
11.18: And you will trust, because there is hope; you will look about you and lie down in safety.
11.19: You will lie down, and none will disturb you; many will entreat your favor.
11.20: But the eyes of the wicked will fail; all escape will be denied them, and their hope is the giving up of the ghost.

12.2: Truly, you are the people, and wisdom will die with you!
12.3: But I have understanding as well as you; I am not inferior to you. Who does not know such things as these?
12.4: I am become a laughingstock to my neighbor — I who called to God and he answered me — the righteous and blameless man is a laughingstock.
12.5: A torch is despised in the thought of one at ease, ready for those whose feet are about to slip.
12.6: The tents of robbers are at peace, and those who provoke God live secure — into whose hand God himself brings everything.
12.7: But ask the animals and they will teach you; the birds of the air and they will tell you.
12.8: Or speak to the earth and it will teach you; the fish of the sea will declare it to you.
12.9: Who among all these does not know that the hand of the LORD has done this?
12.10: In his hand is the life of every living thing and the breath of all mortal flesh.
12.11: Does not the ear test words as the palate tastes food?
12.12: With the aged is wisdom, and with length of days comes understanding.
12.13: With him are wisdom and might; he has counsel and understanding.
12.14: If he tears down, it cannot be rebuilt; if he shuts a man in, there is no opening.
12.15: If he withholds the waters, they dry up; if he releases them, they overwhelm the land.
12.16: With him are strength and sound wisdom; the deceived and the deceiver alike belong to him.
12.17: He leads counselors away stripped and makes fools of judges.
12.18: He looses the bond of kings and ties a loincloth around their waist.
12.19: He leads priests away stripped and overthrows those long established in power.
12.20: He removes the speech of the trusted and takes away the discernment of the elders.
12.21: He pours contempt upon nobles and looses the belt of the strong.
12.22: He uncovers deep things out of the darkness and brings the shadow of death into the light.
12.23: He makes nations great and destroys them; he spreads nations abroad and leads them away.
12.24: He takes away the understanding of the chiefs of the peoples of the earth and makes them wander in a trackless waste.
12.25: They grope in the dark without light, and he makes them stagger like a drunken man.

13.1: Lo, all this mine eye hath seen, mine ear hath heard and understood it.
13.2: What ye know, the same do I know also; I am not inferior unto you.
13.3: Surely I would speak to the Almighty, and I desire to reason with God.
13.4: But ye are forgers of lies; ye are all physicians of no value.
13.5: O that ye would altogether hold your peace! And it should be your wisdom.
13.6: Hear now my reasoning, and hearken to the pleadings of my lips.
13.7: Will ye speak wickedly for God? And talk deceitfully for him?
13.8: Will ye show partiality for him? Will ye contend for God?
13.9: Is it good that he should search you out? Or as one deceiveth a man, will ye so deceive him?
13.10: He will surely reprove you if ye do secretly show partiality.
13.11: Shall not his majesty terrify you? And his dread fall upon you?
13.12: Your memorable sayings are proverbs of ashes; your defenses are defenses of clay.
13.13: Hold your peace from me, that I may speak; and let come upon me what will.
13.14: Why should I take my flesh in my teeth, and put my life in my hand?
13.15: Though he slay me, yet will I hope in him; but I will surely defend my own ways before him.
13.16: Even this shall be my salvation, that a godless person shall not come before him.
13.17: Hear diligently my speech, and let my declaration be in your ears.
13.18: Behold now, I have ordered my cause; I know that I shall be justified.
13.19: Who is he that will contend with me? For then I would be silent and give up the ghost.
13.20: Only do not two things unto me; then will I not hide myself from your face.
13.21: Remove your hand far from me, and let not the dread of you terrify me.
13.22: Then call, and I will answer; or let me speak, and you answer me.
13.23: How many are my iniquities and my sins? Make me know my transgression and my sin.
13.24: Why do you hide your face, and count me as your enemy?
13.25: Will you frighten a windblown leaf? And will you chase dry stubble?
13.26: For you write bitter things against me, and make me to inherit the iniquities of my youth.
13.27: You put my feet in the stocks, and watch all my paths; you trace the prints of my feet.
13.28: And he wastes away like a rotten thing, like a garment that is moth-eaten.

14.1: Man born of woman is few of days and full of trouble.
14.2: He comes forth like a flower and withers; he flees like a shadow and does not last.
14.3: And upon such a one do you fix your eyes, and bring him into judgment before you?
14.4: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? Not one.
14.5: Since his days are determined, the number of his months is with you; you have set his bounds that he cannot pass —
14.6: — look away from him so he may rest, till he has served out his day like a hired hand.
14.7: For there is hope for a tree — if it is cut down it will sprout again, and its shoots will not fail.
14.8: Though its root grows old in the earth and its stump dies in the ground —
14.9: — yet at the scent of water it will bud and bring forth branches like a young plant.
14.10: But man dies and is laid low; man breathes his last, and where is he?
14.11: As waters fail from the sea, and the river decays and dries up —
14.12: — so man lies down and rises not; till the heavens are no more they will not awake, nor be roused from their sleep.
14.13: O that you would hide me in Sheol, that you would conceal me until your wrath is past, that you would set me a fixed time and remember me!
14.14: If a man dies, shall he live again? All the days of my appointed service I will wait, until my relief comes.
14.15: You will call, and I will answer you; you will long for the work of your hands.
14.16: For now you number my steps; you do not fail to watch over my sin.
14.17: My transgression is sealed up in a pouch, and you have plastered over my iniquity.
14.18: But the falling mountain crumbles away, and the rock is moved from its place;
14.19: the waters wear away the stones; its torrents wash away the soil of the earth; and you destroy the hope of man.
14.20: You overpower him forever, and he passes; you change his face and send him away.
14.21: His sons come to honor, and he does not know it; they are brought low, and he does not perceive them.
14.22: He feels only the pain of his own body, and mourns only for himself.

15.1: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said:
15.2: Should a wise man utter vain knowledge, and fill his belly with the east wind?
15.3: Should he reason with unprofitable talk, and with speeches by which he can do no good?
15.4: You even undermine the fear of God and restrain prayer before him.
15.5: For your mouth speaks from your iniquity, and you choose the tongue of the crafty.
15.6: Your own mouth condemns you, and not I; your own lips testify against you.
15.7: Were you the first man ever born? Were you brought forth before the hills?
15.8: Have you listened in on the secret counsel of God? And do you restrain wisdom to yourself?
15.9: What do you know that we do not know? What do you understand that we do not?
15.10: Among us are both the gray-haired and the very aged, older by far than your father.
15.11: Are the consolations of God too small for you, and the gentle word that comes to you?
15.12: Why does your heart carry you away, and why do your eyes flash —
15.13: — that you turn your spirit against God and let such words go out of your mouth?
15.14: What is man, that he can be clean? And he who is born of woman, that he can be righteous?
15.15: Behold, God puts no trust in his holy ones, and the heavens are not clean in his sight.
15.16: How much more abominable and corrupt is man, who drinks iniquity like water!
15.17: I will show you; hear me, and what I have seen I will declare —
15.18: — what wise men have told, and their fathers have not concealed,
15.19: to whom alone the land was given, when no foreigner passed among them —
15.20: The wicked man writhes in pain all his days, and few years are stored up for the ruthless.
15.21: A dreadful sound is in his ears; in his time of peace a destroyer comes upon him.
15.22: He does not believe he will return from darkness, and he is appointed for the sword.
15.23: He wanders abroad for bread, saying, 'Where is it?' He knows that the day of darkness is close at hand.
15.24: Distress and anguish terrify him; they overwhelm him like a king marshaled for battle.
15.25: Because he has stretched out his hand against God and defied the Almighty,
15.26: running against him neck-first, upon the thick bosses of his shields,
15.27: because he has covered his face with his fat and made his flanks thick with flesh —
15.28: he inhabits ruined cities, houses that no one lives in, which are ready to become heaps —
15.29: he will not be rich, nor will his wealth endure, nor will his possessions spread over the earth.
15.30: He will not escape from darkness; flame will dry up his shoots, and by the breath of God's mouth he will be swept away.
15.31: Let him not trust in emptiness, deceiving himself, for emptiness will be his repayment.
15.32: It will be paid off before his day comes, and his branch will not be green.
15.33: He will shake off his unripe grape like the vine, and cast off his blossom like the olive tree.
15.34: For the company of the godless will be barren, and fire will consume the tents of bribery.
15.35: They conceive mischief and bring forth evil, and their inmost parts prepare deceit.

16.2: I have heard many such things; comforters of trouble are you all.
16.3: Shall windy words have an end? Or what emboldens you that you answer?
16.4: I also could speak as you do; if your soul were in my soul's place, I could heap up words against you and shake my head at you.
16.5: I would strengthen you with my mouth, and the consolation of my lips would assuage your pain.
16.6: Though I speak, my pain is not assuaged; and though I hold back, what departs from me?
16.7: Surely now he has made me weary; he has desolated all my company.
16.8: You have shriveled me — which is a witness; and my leanness rises up to testify against me to my face.
16.9: He has torn me in his anger and is hostile to me; he gnashes his teeth at me; my foe sharpens his eyes against me.
16.10: They have gaped at me with their mouth; they have struck me on the cheek in reproach; they have massed together against me.
16.11: God has handed me over to the ungodly and cast me into the hands of the wicked.
16.12: I was at ease, and he shattered me; he seized me by the neck and dashed me to pieces; he set me up as his target.
16.13: His archers encircle me; he pierces my kidneys without mercy and pours my gall upon the ground.
16.14: He breaks me through with breach upon breach; he runs upon me like a warrior.
16.15: I have sewn sackcloth upon my skin and laid my horn in the dust.
16.16: My face is reddened with weeping, and the shadow of death is on my eyelids —
16.17: — though no violence is in my hands, and my prayer is pure.
16.18: O earth, cover not my blood, and let my cry have no resting place!
16.19: Even now my witness is in heaven, and my advocate is on high.
16.20: My friends mock me; but my eye weeps to God.
16.21: O that a man might plead with God as a man pleads for his neighbor —
16.22: — for the years that number are coming, and I shall walk a path from which I will not return.

17.1: My spirit is destroyed; my days are extinguished; the grave is ready for me.
17.2: Are there not mockers with me? And does not my eye dwell on their provocation?
17.3: Lay down a pledge for me with yourself; who else will strike his hand for me?
17.4: For you have hidden their hearts from understanding; therefore you will not exalt them.
17.5: He who denounces his friends for gain — the eyes of his children shall fail.
17.6: He has made me a byword among the peoples, and I am one before whose face men spit.
17.7: My eye has grown dim with grief, and all my limbs are like shadows.
17.8: Upright men are appalled at this, and the innocent stirs himself against the godless.
17.9: The righteous one holds to his way, and the one with clean hands grows ever stronger.
17.10: But all of you, come now — I cannot find a wise man among you.
17.11: My days are past; my plans are broken off, even the deepest longings of my heart.
17.12: They turn night into day; they say, 'The light is near,' even in the face of darkness.
17.13: If I hope, Sheol is my house; I have made my bed in the darkness.
17.14: I say to the pit, 'You are my father,' and to the worm, 'My mother and my sister.'
17.15: Where then is my hope? And who perceives any hope for me?
17.16: Shall it go down with me to the gates of Sheol? Together we shall descend into the dust.

18.1: And Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
18.2: How long will you make an end of words? Consider, and then we will speak.
18.3: Why are we counted as cattle? Why are we vile in your sight?
18.4: You who tear yourself in your anger — will the earth be abandoned for your sake? Or the rock removed from its place?
18.5: Yes, the light of the wicked is put out, and the flame of his fire does not shine.
18.6: The light in his tent is darkened, and the lamp above him is put out.
18.7: His vigorous steps are cramped, and his own counsel throws him down.
18.8: For he is cast into a net by his own feet, and he walks into a snare.
18.9: A trap seizes him by the heel; a snare grips him.
18.10: A rope is hidden in the ground for him, and a trap waits on his path.
18.11: Terrors frighten him on every side and drive him at every step.
18.12: His vigor is famished, and calamity stands ready at his side.
18.13: The parts of his skin are devoured; the firstborn of death devours his limbs.
18.14: His security is torn from his tent, and he is marched to the king of terrors.
18.15: What is not his dwells in his tent; brimstone is scattered over his dwelling.
18.16: Below, his roots dry up; above, his branches wither.
18.17: His memory perishes from the earth, and he has no name in the streets.
18.18: He is pushed from light into darkness and expelled from the world.
18.19: He has no offspring or descendant among his people, and no survivor in the place where he sojourned.
18.20: Those who come after him are appalled at his day, and those who went before him are seized with horror.
18.21: Surely such are the dwellings of the ungodly, and this is the place of one who does not know God.

19.2: How long will ye vex my soul and crush me in pieces with words?
19.3: These ten times ye have reproached me; ye are not ashamed that ye deal hardly with me.
19.4: And be it indeed that I have erred, mine error lodgeth within myself.
19.5: If indeed ye magnify yourselves against me and plead my disgrace against me,
19.6: know then that God hath overthrown me and compassed me about with his net.
19.7: Behold, I cry out of wrong but I am not heard; I cry aloud but there is no judgment.
19.8: He hath fenced up my way that I cannot pass, and he hath set darkness upon my paths.
19.9: He hath stripped me of my glory and taken the crown from my head.
19.10: He hath broken me down on every side, and I am gone; mine hope hath he pulled up like a tree.
19.11: He hath also kindled his anger against me, and he counteth me unto him as one of his enemies.
19.12: His troops come together and raise their way against me; they encamp round about my tabernacle.
19.13: He hath put my brethren far from me, and mine acquaintance are verily estranged from me.
19.14: My kinsfolk have failed and my familiar friends have forgotten me.
19.15: They that dwell in mine house and my maids count me for a stranger; I am an alien in their sight.
19.16: I called unto my servant, and he gave me no answer; I entreated him with my mouth.
19.17: My breath is offensive to my wife, and I am loathsome to the children of my own body.
19.18: Even young children despise me; I arise and they speak against me.
19.19: All my intimate friends abhor me, and they whom I loved are turned against me.
19.20: My bone cleaveth to my skin and to my flesh, and I am escaped with the skin of my teeth.
19.21: Have pity upon me, have pity upon me, O ye my friends, for the hand of God hath touched me.
19.22: Why do ye persecute me as God, and are not satisfied with my flesh?
19.23: Oh that my words were now written! Oh that they were inscribed in a book!
19.24: That they were graven with an iron pen and lead in the rock for ever!
19.25: For I know that my Redeemer liveth, and that he shall stand at the last upon the dust.
19.26: And after my skin hath been thus destroyed, yet from within my flesh I shall see God,
19.27: whom I myself shall see, and mine own eyes shall behold, and not a stranger; my heart faileth within me.
19.28: If ye should say, 'Why do we persecute him?' seeing the root of the matter is found in him —
19.29: — be ye afraid of the sword; for wrath bringeth the punishment of the sword, that ye may know there is a judgment.

20.1: Then Zophar the Naamathite answered and said:
20.2: Therefore do my thoughts cause me to answer, and because of this I make haste.
20.3: I have heard the check of my reproach, and a spirit beyond my understanding causeth me to answer.
20.4: Knowest thou not this of old, since man was placed upon the earth,
20.5: that the triumphing of the wicked is short, and the joy of the hypocrite but for a moment?
20.6: Though his excellency mount up to the heavens and his head reach unto the clouds —
20.7: — he shall perish for ever like his own dung; they which have seen him shall say, 'Where is he?'
20.8: He shall fly away as a dream, and shall not be found; yea, he shall be chased away as a vision of the night.
20.9: The eye also which saw him shall see him no more; neither shall his place any more behold him.
20.10: His children shall seek to please the poor, and his hands shall restore their goods.
20.11: His bones are full of the sin of his youth, which shall lie down with him in the dust.
20.12: Though wickedness be sweet in his mouth, though he hide it under his tongue —
20.13: — though he spare it, and forsake it not, but keep it still within his mouth —
20.14: — yet his meat is turned in his bowels; it is the gall of asps within him.
20.15: He hath swallowed down riches, and shall vomit them up again; God shall cast them out of his belly.
20.16: He shall suck the poison of asps; the viper's tongue shall slay him.
20.17: He shall not see the rivers, the floods, the brooks of honey and butter.
20.18: That which he laboured for shall he restore, and shall not swallow it down; according to his substance shall the restitution be, and he shall not rejoice.
20.19: Because he hath oppressed and forsaken the poor; because he hath violently seized a house which he builded not.
20.20: Surely he shall not feel quietness in his belly; he shall not save of that which he desired.
20.21: There shall none of his meat be left; therefore shall no man look for his goods.
20.22: In the fulness of his sufficiency he shall be in straits; every hand of the wicked shall come upon him.
20.23: When he is about to fill his belly, God shall cast the fury of his wrath upon him and rain it upon him while he is eating.
20.24: He shall flee from the iron weapon, and the bow of bronze shall strike him through.
20.25: The arrow is drawn and cometh out of his body; the flashing point passeth through his gall; terrors are upon him.
20.26: All darkness is stored up for his treasures; a fire not blown shall consume him; it shall go ill with him that is left in his tabernacle.
20.27: The heaven shall reveal his iniquity, and the earth shall rise up against him.
20.28: The increase of his house shall depart, and his goods shall flow away in the day of his anger.
20.29: This is the portion of a wicked man from God, and the heritage appointed to him by God.

21.2: Hear diligently my speech, and let this be your consolations.
21.3: Suffer me that I may speak; and after that I have spoken, mock on.
21.4: As for me, is my complaint to man? And if it were so, why should not my spirit be impatient?
21.5: Mark me, and be astonished, and lay your hand upon your mouth.
21.6: Even when I remember I am troubled, and trembling seizeth my flesh.
21.7: Wherefore do the wicked live, grow old, yea, grow mighty in power?
21.8: Their seed is established with them before their faces, and their offspring before their eyes.
21.9: Their houses are safe from fear, neither is the rod of God upon them.
21.10: Their bull gendereth and faileth not; their cow calveth and casteth not her calf.
21.11: They send forth their little ones like a flock, and their children dance.
21.12: They take the timbrel and harp, and rejoice at the sound of the pipe.
21.13: They spend their days in prosperity, and in a moment go down to Sheol.
21.14: Yet they say to God, 'Depart from us; we do not desire the knowledge of thy ways.'
21.15: Who is the Almighty that we should serve him? And what profit have we if we pray to him?
21.16: Lo, their prosperity is not in their own hand; the counsel of the wicked is far from me.
21.17: How oft is the lamp of the wicked put out? And how oft cometh their calamity upon them? God distributeth sorrows in his anger.
21.18: They are as straw before the wind, and as chaff that the storm carrieth away.
21.19: God layeth up his iniquity for his children; he rewardeth him and he shall know it.
21.20: Let his own eyes see his destruction; let him drink of the wrath of the Almighty.
21.21: For what pleasure hath he in his house after him, when the number of his months is cut off?
21.22: Shall any teach God knowledge? seeing he judgeth those that are high.
21.23: One dieth in his full strength, being wholly at ease and at rest,
21.24: his sides full of milk and his bones moistened with marrow.
21.25: And another dieth in the bitterness of his soul, and never eateth with pleasure.
21.26: They shall lie down alike in the dust, and the worms shall cover them.
21.27: Behold, I know your thoughts and the devices which ye wrongfully imagine against me.
21.28: For ye say, 'Where is the house of the prince? And where are the dwelling-places of the wicked?'
21.29: Have ye not asked them that go by the way? And do ye not know their tokens?
21.30: That the wicked is spared in the day of calamity and is brought through to the day of wrath.
21.31: Who shall declare his way to his face? And who shall repay him what he hath done?
21.32: Yet shall he be carried to the grave, and a watch shall be kept over his tomb.
21.33: The clods of the valley are sweet to him, and every man shall follow after him, as before him was a countless number.
21.34: How then comfort ye me in vain, seeing in your answers there remaineth falsehood?

25.1: And Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
25.2: Dominion and dread are with him; he maketh peace in his high places.
25.3: Is there any number to his armies? And upon whom doth not his light arise?
25.4: How then can a man be justified with God? Or how can he be clean who is born of a woman?
25.5: Behold, even the moon shineth not, and the stars are not pure in his sight.
25.6: How much less man, that is a maggot, and the son of man, that is a worm!

26.2: How hast thou helped him that is without power! How hast thou saved the arm that hath no strength!
26.3: How hast thou counselled him that hath no wisdom! And how plentifully hast thou declared sound understanding!
26.4: To whom hast thou uttered words? And whose spirit came out from thee?
26.5: The shades tremble beneath the waters and those that dwell under them.
26.6: Sheol is naked before him, and Abaddon hath no covering.
26.7: He stretcheth out the north over empty space and hangeth the earth upon nothing.
26.8: He bindeth up the waters in his thick clouds, and the cloud is not rent under them.
26.9: He holdeth back the face of his throne and spreadeth his cloud over it.
26.10: He hath drawn a circular bound upon the face of the waters at the boundary of light and darkness.
26.11: The pillars of heaven tremble and are astonished at his rebuke.
26.12: By his power he stilleth the sea; by his understanding he smiteth through Rahab.
26.13: By his Spirit the heavens were adorned; his hand hath pierced the fleeing serpent.
26.14: Lo, these are the outskirts of his ways; and how faint a whisper is heard of him! The thunder of his power — who can understand it?

27.1: Moreover Job continued his parable and said:
27.2: As God liveth, who hath taken away my right, and the Almighty, who hath made my soul bitter —
27.3: All the while my breath is in me, and the spirit of God is in my nostrils —
27.4: My lips shall not speak wickedness, nor my tongue utter deceit.
27.5: Far be it from me to justify you; till I die I will not remove mine integrity from me.
27.6: My righteousness I hold fast and will not let it go; my heart reproacheth me not for any of my days.
27.7: Let mine enemy be as the wicked and he that riseth up against me as the unrighteous.
27.8: For what is the hope of the hypocrite though he gain, when God taketh away his soul?
27.9: Will God hear his cry when trouble cometh upon him?
27.10: Will he delight himself in the Almighty? Will he always call upon God?
27.11: I will teach you concerning the hand of God; that which is with the Almighty I will not conceal.
27.12: Behold, all ye yourselves have seen it; why then are ye altogether vain?
27.13: This is the portion of a wicked man with God and the heritage of oppressors which they receive of the Almighty.
27.14: If his children be multiplied, it is for the sword; and his offspring shall not be satisfied with bread.
27.15: Those that remain of him shall be buried in death, and his widows shall not weep.
27.16: Though he heap up silver as the dust and prepare raiment as the clay —
27.17: He may prepare it, but the just shall put it on, and the innocent shall divide the silver.
27.18: He buildeth his house as a moth buildeth it, and as a booth that the watchman maketh.
27.19: The rich man lieth down and shall not be gathered again; he openeth his eyes, and is not.
27.20: Terrors overtake him like waters; a tempest stealeth him away in the night.
27.21: The east wind carrieth him away and he departeth; and a storm hurleth him out of his place.
27.22: For God shall cast upon him and not spare; he would fain flee out of his hand.
27.23: Men shall clap their hands at him and shall hiss him out of his place.

28.1: Surely there is a mine for the silver and a place for gold where they refine it.
28.2: Iron is taken out of the earth, and copper is molten out of the stone.
28.3: He setteth a limit to the darkness and searcheth out to its furthest reach the stone in thick darkness and in the shadow of death.
28.4: A shaft is broken open far from men's dwellings; forgotten by the foot of travelers, they hang far from others, they swing.
28.5: As for the earth, out of it cometh bread, but under it is turned up as it were fire.
28.6: The stones of it are the place of sapphires, and it hath dust of gold.
28.7: A path that no bird of prey knoweth and which the falcon's eye hath not seen.
28.8: The lion's whelps have not trodden it, nor the fierce lion passed by it.
28.9: He putteth forth his hand upon the flint; he overturneth mountains by the roots.
28.10: He cutteth out channels among the rocks; his eye seeth every precious thing.
28.11: He bindeth the streams from overflowing, and the thing that is hidden he bringeth to light.
28.12: But where shall wisdom be found, and where is the place of understanding?
28.13: Man knoweth not the price thereof, neither is it found in the land of the living.
28.14: The deep saith, It is not in me; and the sea saith, It is not with me.
28.15: It cannot be gotten for gold, neither shall silver be weighed for the price thereof.
28.16: It cannot be valued with the gold of Ophir, with the precious onyx or the sapphire.
28.17: Gold and crystal cannot equal it, nor shall the exchange for it be in vessels of fine gold.
28.18: No mention shall be made of coral or of crystal; for the price of wisdom is above rubies.
28.19: The topaz of Ethiopia shall not equal it, neither shall it be valued with pure gold.
28.20: Whence then cometh wisdom, and where is the place of understanding?
28.21: Seeing it is hid from the eyes of all the living and concealed from the fowls of the air.
28.22: Abaddon and Death say, We have heard a rumor of it with our ears.
28.23: God understandeth the way thereof and he knoweth the place thereof.
28.24: For he looketh to the ends of the earth and seeth under the whole heaven.
28.25: When he made a weight for the wind and measured out the waters by measure —
28.26: When he made a decree for the rain and a path for the lightning of the thunder —
28.27: Then did he see it and declare it; he prepared it and searched it out fully.
28.28: And unto man he said: Behold, the fear of the Lord — that is wisdom; and to depart from evil is understanding.

22.1: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said:
22.2: Can a man be profitable to God? Even the wise man profits only himself.
22.3: Is it any pleasure to the Almighty that you are righteous, or any gain that you make your ways blameless?
22.4: Is it because of your piety that he reproves you and brings you into judgment?
22.5: Is not your wickedness great? Your iniquities without end?
22.6: For you have taken pledges from your brothers without cause and stripped the naked of their clothing.
22.7: You have not given water to the weary to drink, and you have withheld bread from the hungry.
22.8: The man of power had the earth, and the man of rank dwelt in it.
22.9: You have sent widows away empty, and the arms of the fatherless have been crushed.
22.10: Therefore snares surround you, and sudden dread throws you into panic.
22.11: Or darkness so thick that you cannot see, and a flood of water overwhelming you.
22.12: Is not God high in the heavens? Look how high the stars are exalted.
22.13: And you say, 'What does God know? Can he judge through the dense cloud?'
22.14: Dense clouds cover him so that he does not see; he walks on the vault of heaven.
22.15: Will you keep to the ancient path that wicked men have walked —
22.16: — those who were snatched away before their time, their foundation swept away by a flood?
22.17: They said to God, 'Turn away from us' — and 'What can the Almighty do to us?'
22.18: Yet he had filled their houses with good things. But the counsel of the wicked is far from me.
22.19: The righteous see their ruin and rejoice; the innocent mock them:
22.20: Surely our adversaries have been cut off, and what remained of them fire has consumed.
22.21: Agree with him now, and be at peace; thereby good will come to you.
22.22: Receive the law from his mouth; lay up his words in your heart.
22.23: If you return to the Almighty, you will be built up; if you put iniquity far from your tents,
22.24: then lay up your gold as dust, and the gold of Ophir among the stones of the wadis;
22.25: and the Almighty will be your gold and your gleaming silver.
22.26: For then you will delight yourself in the Almighty and lift your face to God.
22.27: You will pray to him and he will hear you; you will fulfill your vows.
22.28: Whatever you decide will be established for you, and light will shine on your ways.
22.29: When men are cast down you will say, 'There is lifting up'; and he will save the downcast in spirit.
22.30: He delivers even one who is not innocent; he will be delivered through the cleanness of your hands.

23.2: Even today my complaint is bitter; his hand is heavier than my groaning.
23.3: Oh that I knew where I might find him — that I might come to his dwelling place!
23.4: I would set my cause in order before him and fill my mouth with arguments.
23.5: I would know what he would answer me, and understand what he would say to me.
23.6: Would he contend with me in the greatness of his power? No — he would give heed to me.
23.7: There an upright man could argue with him; and I would be delivered once and for all from my judge.
23.8: But I go forward, and he is not there; and backward, but I cannot perceive him;
23.9: on the left where he works, but I cannot behold him; he turns to the right, but I cannot see him.
23.10: But he knows the way I take; when he has tested me, I shall come forth as gold.
23.11: My foot has held fast to his steps; I have kept his way and not turned aside.
23.12: From the commandment of his lips I have not departed; I have treasured the words of his mouth more than my portion of food.
23.13: But he is of one mind, and who can turn him? What his soul desires, that he does.
23.14: For he will complete what is appointed for me; and many such things are with him.
23.15: Therefore I am terrified at his presence; when I consider, I am shaken before him.
23.16: God has made my heart faint, and the Almighty has dismayed me;
23.17: yet I am not silenced by the darkness, nor by the thick gloom that covers my face.

24.1: Why are times not stored up by the Almighty, so that those who know him see his days of judgment?
24.2: There are those who remove boundary markers; they seize flocks by force and graze them.
24.3: They drive off the donkey of the fatherless; they take the widow's ox as a pledge.
24.4: They thrust the needy off the road; the poor of the earth all go into hiding together.
24.5: Behold, like wild donkeys in the desert they go to their labor, seeking prey in the wasteland as food for their children.
24.6: In the field they reap fodder that is not their own; they glean in the vineyard of the wicked.
24.7: They spend the night naked without clothing, with no covering in the cold.
24.8: They are drenched by the mountain showers and cling to the rock for want of shelter.
24.9: They tear the fatherless child from the breast; they seize a pledge from the poor.
24.10: They go about naked without clothing; hungry, they carry the sheaves.
24.11: Between the walls they press out oil; they tread the winepresses, yet suffer thirst.
24.12: From the city men groan; the soul of the wounded cries out — yet God charges no one with wrong.
24.13: They are among those who rebel against the light; they do not know its ways, nor do they stay in its paths.
24.14: With the first light the murderer rises; he kills the poor and needy; in the night he acts as a thief.
24.15: The adulterer's eye watches for the twilight, saying, 'No eye will see me,' and he covers his face.
24.16: In the dark they dig through houses; by day they seal themselves in — they have no dealings with the light.
24.17: For the shadow of death is their morning; they are at home with the terrors of deep darkness.
24.18: Swift is he on the surface of the water; cursed is their portion in the land; he does not turn toward the vineyards.
24.19: Drought and heat snatch away the snow waters; so does Sheol those who have sinned.
24.20: The womb forgets him; the worm feeds on him with appetite; he will not be remembered; wickedness is snapped like a tree.
24.21: He preys on the barren woman who bears no child, and does no good to the widow.
24.22: He drags away the mighty with his strength; he rises up, and none can be sure of life.
24.23: Though he is given safety and he rests on it, yet his eyes are upon all their ways.
24.24: They are exalted for a little while, and then are gone; they are brought low and gathered in like all others; they are cut off like the tops of the grain.
24.25: And if it is not so, who will prove me a liar and make my speech amount to nothing?

29.1: Moreover Job continued his parable and said:
29.2: Oh that I were as in months past, as in the days when God preserved me!
29.3: when his lamp shone upon my head, and by his light I walked through darkness;
29.4: as I was in the days of my youth, when the friendship of God was over my tent;
29.5: when Shaddai was yet with me, and my children were around me;
29.6: when I washed my steps with cream, and the rock poured out rivers of oil for me;
29.7: when I went out to the gate of the city, when I took my seat in the square;
29.8: the young men saw me and withdrew, and the aged rose and stood;
29.9: the princes refrained from speaking and laid their hands on their mouths;
29.10: the voice of the nobles was hushed, and their tongues cleaved to the roof of their mouths.
29.11: For the ear that heard me blessed me, and the eye that saw me bore witness for me;
29.12: because I delivered the poor who cried out, and the fatherless who had no one to help them.
29.13: The blessing of him that was about to perish came upon me, and I caused the widow's heart to sing for joy.
29.14: I clothed myself with righteousness and it clothed me; my justice was as a robe and a turban.
29.15: I was eyes to the blind, and I was feet to the lame.
29.16: I was a father to the poor, and the case of the one I did not know I searched out.
29.17: I broke the fangs of the wicked and wrested the prey from his teeth.
29.18: Then I said, 'I shall die in my own nest, and I will multiply my days like the sand.'
29.19: My root spread out to the waters, and the dew lay all night upon my branch;
29.20: my glory was ever fresh within me, and my bow was ever renewed in my hand.
29.21: Men listened to me and waited and kept silence for my counsel.
29.22: After my words they spoke no more; and my speech dropped gently upon them.
29.23: They waited for me as for the rain, and they opened their mouths wide as for the spring rain.
29.24: If I smiled upon them, they could scarcely believe it; and the light of my face they did not let fall.
29.25: I chose the course for them and sat as chief; I dwelt as a king among his troops, as one who comforts the mourners.

30.1: But now those younger than I have me in derision, men whose fathers I would have refused to put with the dogs of my flock.
30.2: Yes, what is the strength of their hands to me, men in whom vigor has perished?
30.3: Through want and famine they were gaunt; they fled to the wilderness, desolate and waste.
30.4: Who gathered saltbush among the shrubs, and the root of the broom plant was their food.
30.5: They were driven out from among men; they were shouted after as after a thief.
30.6: They were made to dwell in the gullies of valleys, in holes of the earth, among the rocks.
30.7: Among the bushes they brayed; beneath the nettles they huddled together.
30.8: Children of senseless people, children of nameless men, they were beaten out of the land.
30.9: And now I have become their song; I am a byword to them.
30.10: They abhor me, they keep far from me, and do not hesitate to spit in my face.
30.11: Because he has loosed my cord and afflicted me, they have thrown off all restraint in my presence.
30.12: On my right the rabble rises; they drive away my feet and build against me their roads of ruin.
30.13: They destroy my path; they advance my ruin; they have no one to help them — they need none.
30.14: They come in as through a wide breach; amid the crash they roll in.
30.15: Terrors are turned upon me; my dignity is swept away like the wind, and my welfare passes like a cloud.
30.16: And now my soul is poured out within me; days of affliction grip me.
30.17: At night my bones are pierced with pain, and the gnawing pain within me does not rest.
30.18: With great force my garment is seized; it binds me like the collar of my coat.
30.19: He has cast me into the mud, and I have become like dust and ashes.
30.20: I cry out to you and you do not answer; I stand up and you only look at me.
30.21: You have turned cruel to me; with the strength of your hand you press hard against me.
30.22: You lift me up to the wind; you make me ride upon it, and you toss me in the storm.
30.23: For I know that you will bring me to death, and to the house appointed for all the living.
30.24: Yet does not one in a heap of ruins reach out his hand? Does a man not cry for help in his calamity?
30.25: Did I not weep for him whose day was hard? Was not my soul grieved for the poor?
30.26: But when I looked for good, evil came; when I waited for the light, darkness came instead.
30.27: My inner parts seethe and will not rest; days of affliction have come before me.
30.28: I go about in gloom without sunlight; I stand up in the assembly and cry out.
30.29: I have become a brother to jackals and a companion to ostriches.
30.30: My skin turns black and peels from me, and my bones burn with fever.
30.31: My harp has turned to mourning, and my flute to the voice of those who weep.

31.1: A covenant I made with mine eyes; and how then should I gaze upon a virgin?
31.2: For what is the portion of God from above, and the heritage of the Almighty from on high?
31.3: Is not destruction for the unjust and calamity for the workers of iniquity?
31.4: Does not he see my ways and count all my steps?
31.5: If I have walked in vanity, or my foot hath hastened unto deceit —
31.6: Let me be weighed in a just balance, that God may know mine integrity.
31.7: If my step hath turned aside from the way, or mine heart hath walked after mine eyes, or any blot hath cleaved to my hands —
31.8: Then let me sow and another eat, and let my offspring be rooted out.
31.9: If mine heart hath been enticed by a woman, or if I have lain in wait at my neighbour's door —
31.10: Then let my wife grind for another and let others bow down over her.
31.11: For that would be a heinous crime, and an iniquity to be judged.
31.12: For it is a fire that consumeth unto Abaddon and would root out all mine increase.
31.13: If I have despised the cause of my manservant or of my maidservant when they contended with me —
31.14: What then shall I do when God riseth up? And when he visiteth, what shall I answer him?
31.15: Did not he that made me in the womb make him? And did not one fashion us in the womb?
31.16: If I have withheld the poor from their desire, or have caused the eyes of the widow to fail —
31.17: Or have eaten my morsel alone, and the fatherless hath not eaten thereof —
31.18: For from my youth he grew up with me as with a father, and from my mother's womb I guided the widow.
31.19: If I have seen any perish for lack of clothing or the poor without covering —
31.20: If his loins have not blessed me, and if he was not warmed with the fleece of my sheep —
31.21: If I have lifted up my hand against the fatherless when I saw my help in the gate —
31.22: Then let mine arm fall from the shoulder blade and mine arm be broken at the elbow.
31.23: For the destruction of God is a terror to me, and by reason of his highness I could not endure.
31.24: If I have made gold my hope or said to fine gold, Thou art my confidence —
31.25: If I rejoiced because my wealth was great and because mine hand had gotten much —
31.26: If I beheld the sun when it shone or the moon walking in brightness —
31.27: And my heart was secretly enticed and my mouth hath kissed my hand —
31.28: This also were an iniquity to be judged, for I should have denied the God who is above.
31.29: If I rejoiced at the destruction of him who hated me or exulted when evil found him —
31.30: Neither have I allowed my mouth to sin by asking a curse upon his soul —
31.31: If the men of my household said not, Who is there that hath not been satisfied with his meat? —
31.32: The stranger did not lodge in the street; I opened my doors to the traveller.
31.33: If I have covered my transgressions as Adam, by hiding mine iniquity in my bosom —
31.34: Because I feared the great multitude and the contempt of families terrified me, so that I kept silence and did not go out of the door —
31.35: Oh that one would hear me! Behold, my mark — let the Almighty answer me; and that mine adversary had written a book!
31.36: Surely I would carry it upon my shoulder and bind it as a crown upon my head.
31.37: I would declare unto him the number of my steps; as a prince I would approach him.
31.38: If my land crieth out against me and its furrows weep together —
31.39: If I have eaten its fruits without payment or have caused the owners thereof to lose their life —
31.40: Let thistles grow instead of wheat and weeds instead of barley. The words of Job are ended.

32.1: So these three men ceased to answer Job because he was righteous in his own eyes.
32.2: Then was the anger of Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram, kindled; against Job his anger was kindled because he justified himself rather than God.
32.3: Also against his three friends was his anger kindled because they had found no answer and yet had condemned Job.
32.4: Now Elihu had waited to speak with Job because they were older than he.
32.5: When Elihu saw that there was no answer in the mouth of these three men, his anger was kindled.
32.6: And Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite answered and said: I am young and ye are aged; therefore I was afraid and durst not declare to you mine opinion.
32.7: I said: Days should speak, and multitude of years should teach wisdom.
32.8: But there is a spirit in man, and the breath of the Almighty giveth them understanding.
32.9: The great are not always wise; neither do the aged understand judgment.
32.10: Therefore I said: Hearken to me; I also will declare mine opinion.
32.11: Behold, I waited for your words; I gave ear to your reasons whilst ye searched out what to say.
32.12: Yea, I attended unto you and behold, there was none of you that convinced Job or that answered his words.
32.13: Lest ye say: We have found wisdom — God shall vanquish him, not man.
32.14: Now he hath not directed words against me, and I will not answer him with your speeches.
32.15: They were amazed; they answered no more; words left them.
32.16: When I waited and they did not speak, but stood still and answered no more —
32.17: I said: I will also answer my part; I also will declare mine opinion.
32.18: For I am full of words; the Spirit within me constraineth me.
32.19: Behold, my belly is as wine with no vent; it is ready to burst like new wineskins.
32.20: I will speak that I may find relief; I will open my lips and answer.
32.21: Let me not, I pray, accept any man's person, nor give flattering titles unto any man.
32.22: For I know not how to give flattering titles; in so doing my Maker would soon take me away.

33.1: Wherefore, Job, I pray thee, hear my speeches and hearken to all my words.
33.2: Behold now, I have opened my mouth; my tongue hath spoken in my mouth.
33.3: My words come from the uprightness of my heart, and my lips speak knowledge clearly.
33.4: The Spirit of God hath made me, and the breath of the Almighty hath given me life.
33.5: If thou canst answer me, set thy words in order before me, stand up.
33.6: Behold, I am in God's place as thou didst wish; I also was formed from clay.
33.7: Behold, my terror shall not make thee afraid, nor shall my hand be heavy upon thee.
33.8: Surely thou hast spoken in mine hearing, and I have heard the voice of thy words:
33.9: I am clean, without transgression; I am innocent and there is no iniquity in me.
33.10: Behold, he findeth occasions against me; he counteth me as his enemy.
33.11: He putteth my feet in the stocks; he marketh all my paths.
33.12: Behold, in this thou art not just; I will answer thee: God is greater than man.
33.13: Why dost thou strive against him? For he giveth not account of any of his matters.
33.14: For God speaketh once, yea twice, yet man perceiveth it not.
33.15: In a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep falleth upon men, in slumberings upon the bed —
33.16: Then he openeth the ears of men and sealeth their instruction —
33.17: That he may withdraw man from his purpose and conceal pride from man —
33.18: He keepeth back his soul from the pit and his life from perishing by the sword.
33.19: He is chastened also with pain upon his bed and with the strife of many of his bones —
33.20: So that his life abhorreth bread and his soul dainty meat.
33.21: His flesh is consumed so that it cannot be seen, and his bones that were not seen stick out.
33.22: Yea, his soul draweth near unto the pit and his life to the destroyers.
33.23: If there be a messenger with him, an interpreter, one among a thousand, to declare to man his uprightness —
33.24: Then he is gracious unto him and saith: Deliver him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom.
33.25: His flesh shall be fresher than a child's; he shall return to the days of his youth.
33.26: He shall pray unto God and he will be favourable unto him, and he shall see his face with joy; and he will restore to man his righteousness.
33.27: He looketh upon men, and if any say: I have sinned and perverted what was right, and it profited me not —
33.28: He will deliver his soul from going into the pit and his life shall see the light.
33.29: Lo, all these things God worketh oftentimes with man —
33.30: To bring back his soul from the pit, to be enlightened with the light of the living.
33.31: Mark well, O Job, hearken unto me; hold thy peace and I will speak.
33.32: If thou hast any thing to say, answer me; speak, for I desire to justify thee.
33.33: If not, hearken unto me; hold thy peace and I will teach thee wisdom.

40.1: Moreover the LORD answered Job and said:
40.2: Shall a fault-finder contend with the Almighty? He who reproves God, let him answer it.
40.3: Then Job answered the LORD and said:
40.4: Behold, I am small; what shall I answer you? I lay my hand upon my mouth.
40.5: Once I have spoken and I will not answer; twice, and I will go no further.
40.6: Then the LORD answered Job from the whirlwind and said:
40.7: Gird up your loins now like a man; I will question you, and you shall declare to me.
40.8: Will you nullify my justice? Will you condemn me so that you may be in the right?
40.9: Have you an arm like God? Can you thunder with a voice like his?
40.10: Clothe yourself now with majesty and splendor; array yourself with glory and beauty.
40.11: Pour out the overflow of your anger; look on everyone who is proud and bring him low.
40.12: Look on everyone who is proud and bring him low; crush the wicked in their place.
40.13: Bury them in the dust together; bind their faces in the hidden place.
40.14: Then I will also acknowledge to you that your own right hand can save you.
40.15: Look now at Behemoth, which I made along with you; he eats grass like an ox.
40.16: See now, his strength is in his loins and his power in the muscles of his belly.
40.17: He bends his tail like a cedar; the sinews of his thighs are knit together.
40.18: His bones are like tubes of bronze; his limbs are like bars of iron.
40.19: He is the first of the works of God; only his Maker can bring a sword near him.
40.20: Surely the mountains yield food for him; all the wild beasts of the field play nearby.
40.21: He lies under the lotus trees, in the covert of the reeds and the swamp.
40.22: The lotus trees cover him with their shade; the willows of the brook surround him.
40.23: Though a river rages, he is not alarmed; he is confident though the Jordan surges against his mouth.
40.24: Can one take him by his eyes? Can one pierce his nose with a snare?

41.1: Can you draw out Leviathan with a fishhook, or press down his tongue with a cord?
41.2: Can you put a reed through his nose or pierce his jaw with a hook?
41.3: Will he make many pleas to you? Will he speak gentle words to you?
41.4: Will he make a covenant with you? Will you take him as your servant forever?
41.5: Will you play with him as with a bird? Will you put him on a leash for your girls?
41.6: Will traders bargain over him? Will merchants divide him up for sale?
41.7: Can you fill his skin with barbed irons, or his head with fishing spears?
41.8: Lay your hand on him — remember the battle — do not attempt it again.
41.9: Any hope of subduing him is false; will not one be thrown down at the mere sight of him?
41.10: No one is bold enough to stir him up; who then can stand before me?
41.11: Who has given to me first, that I should repay him? Whatever is under all the heaven is mine.
41.12: I will not keep silent about his limbs, his power, or the grace of his frame.
41.13: Who can strip off his outer covering? Who can penetrate his double armor?
41.14: Who can open the doors of his face? Around his teeth is terror.
41.15: His rows of scales are his pride, sealed tight as with a close seal.
41.16: Each is so close to the next that no air can pass between them.
41.17: They are joined one to another; they grip each other and cannot be separated.
41.18: His sneezes flash with light; his eyes glow like the rays of dawn.
41.19: From his mouth leap burning torches; sparks of fire shoot out.
41.20: Smoke pours from his nostrils as from a boiling pot heated by burning reeds.
41.21: His breath sets coals ablaze; a flame blazes from his mouth.
41.22: In his neck abides strength; before him terror leaps.
41.23: The folds of his flesh cling together; they are firm on him and will not give way.
41.24: His heart is hard as stone, hard as the lower millstone.
41.25: When he rises, the mighty are terrified; they recoil at his crashing.
41.26: No sword that strikes him will hold; neither spear, dart, nor javelin.
41.27: He counts iron as straw and bronze as rotted wood.
41.28: No arrow can make him flee; sling stones are nothing but stubble to him.
41.29: War clubs are counted as straw; he laughs at the rattling of the javelin.
41.30: His underside is like sharp potsherds; he leaves a threshing track in the mud.
41.31: He makes the deep boil like a pot; he makes the sea like a jar of ointment.
41.32: He leaves a shining wake behind him; one would think the deep has turned white-haired.
41.33: On earth there is nothing like him; he is a creature made without fear.
41.34: He looks down on everything that is lofty; he is king over all the sons of pride.

42.1: Then Job answered the LORD and said:
42.2: I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted.
42.3: Who is this who hides counsel without knowledge? Therefore I have spoken of things I did not understand, things too wonderful for me that I did not know.
42.4: You said: Hear, and I will speak; I will question you, and you shall declare to me.
42.5: I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you.
42.6: Therefore I abhor what I said and repent in dust and ashes.
42.7: And it came to pass, after the LORD had spoken these words to Job, that the LORD said to Eliphaz the Temanite: My wrath is kindled against you and against your two friends, for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has.
